title: Sabadilla coulteri
intro: Sabadilla coulteri, also known as Mexican lilyturf or Coulter's sabadilla, is a perennial plant species belonging to the lilyturf family (Scolecocaceae). The plant has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, particularly in treating parasitic infections, such as hookworms and roundworms, due to its anthelmintic properties, which help to eliminate worms and other parasites from the body. The bioactive constituents of Sabadilla coulteri include alkaloids, particularly veratridine and cevadine, which are responsible for its anthelmintic and other pharmacological effects. Traditional herbal preparations made from Sabadilla coulteri include tinctures and infusions, which are often used to treat intestinal parasites and related gastrointestinal issues.
taxonomy: Sabadilla coulteri belongs to the family Ranunculaceae. It is a plant species native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The plant is also known as Mexican sabadilla or Coulter's sabadilla. Sabadilla coulteri is a perennial herb that grows in dry areas, often found in rocky outcrops and scrublands. This plant is a member of the genus Calystegia, which includes several species of bindweed and morning glory.
uses: Sabadilla coulteri has been used in traditional medicine for its antiparasitic and anti-inflammatory properties, particularly in treating skin conditions such as eczema and dermatitis. The plant's extracts have been used to treat various health issues, including intestinal worms and fungal infections. In some cultures, sabadilla coulteri is also used as a natural remedy for respiratory problems, such as bronchitis and coughs. Its active compounds, including alkaloids and glycosides, have been found to have antimicrobial and antifungal properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of sabadilla coulteri.

parts: Sabadilla coulteri, a plant species of the family Ranunculaceae, contains toxic alkaloids that can be extracted for medicinal purposes. The toxic compounds found in Sabadilla coulteri have been traditionally used as an insecticide and for its supposed medicinal properties, although its use is not widely accepted in modern medicine. The plant's root, in particular, is considered the source of its medicinal properties. The root is rich in alkaloids, including veratridine and cevadine, which are responsible for its toxic and medicinal effects. However, due to its toxicity, the use of Sabadilla coulteri as a medicinal plant is highly regulated and not recommended without proper medical supervision.
preparations: Sabadilla coulteri herbal preparations typically involve the use of its seeds, which have been used in traditional medicine for their antispasmodic and insecticidal properties. The seeds are often extracted in the form of an oil or tincture, which can be used to treat a range of ailments, including muscle spasms and skin conditions. In some herbal remedies, the seeds are combined with other plant extracts to enhance their therapeutic effects. The oil extracted from the seeds has been used to repel insects and other pests, making it a popular ingredient in natural pest control products. However, it's essential to note that Sabadilla coulteri preparations should be used under the guidance of a qualified healthcare professional.
side_effects: Sabadilla coulteri contains a toxic alkaloid called veratridine, which can cause severe side effects. Ingestion of the plant can lead to symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. Skin contact with the plant may cause irritation, redness, and inflammation. Long-term exposure to veratridine can result in neurological damage, including muscle weakness, numbness, and tingling sensations. Prolonged consumption of Sabadilla coulteri can also lead to cardiovascular problems, including irregular heartbeat and high blood pressure.
